briefing.headline: 11 high-priority items in the latest EM scan
briefing.summary: Top sources today: European Journal of Emergency Medicine, MedPage Today Emergency Medicine, REBEL EM. Main themes: point-of-care ultrasound, guideline or formal recommendation, trials. Best early reads: Association between patient-to-nurse ratios in emergency departments and patient, staff, and organizational outcomes: a systematic review; Whole Blood Not Better Than Components En Route to Hospital After Traumatic Injury.
briefing.editorialHeadline: Trauma Resuscitation and PE Management: Key Updates
briefing.editorialSummary:
The evidence base continues to refine our approach to high-acuity resuscitation. For major trauma patients anticipated to enter hemorrhagic shock, the TOWAR trial data suggests that administering whole, type O blood en route may be comparably effective to component therapy, challenging long-held prehospital preferences. Meanwhile, in the realm of pulmonary embolism, the HI-PEITHO trial points toward a specific niche: adding ultrasound-guided, catheter-directed fibrinolysis to standard anticoagulation appears to significantly mitigate acute hemodynamic decompensation in the most critically ill, intermediate-risk cohort.

Beyond these major updates, procedural refinement is evident. For acute rib fractures, incorporating a Serratus Anterior Plane Block seems to offer a safe and effective adjunct for improving immediate pain control in the ED. Furthermore, the discussion around PE management is broadening to include localized lysis techniques, suggesting ultrasound-guided, catheter-directed fibrinolysis as a targeted alternative when systemic thrombolysis is suboptimal.

These reads collectively underscore a trend: moving from broad, systemic interventions toward highly targeted, procedural adjuncts—whether it's localized clot dissolution or regional anesthesia for musculoskeletal pain. Paying close attention to these specific, high-yield procedural nuances will sharpen your bedside toolkit this week.

dailyEditorial.title: Association between patient-to-nurse ratios in emergency departments and patient, staff, and organizational outcomes: a systematic review
dailyEditorial.takeaway: While the concept of formal nurse ratios sounds appealing for quality improvement, remember that this systematic review's key findings on ED mortality are not available in the excerpt. Clinicians should be cautious about extrapolating general hospital ratio benefits directly to the ED setting without seeing the full data. For now, treat the evidence on ED-specific mortality reduction from ratios as inconclusive until the full results are reviewed.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This systematic review tackles the perennial question of whether implementing formal patient-to-nurse ratios in the emergency department translates to improved patient outcomes, specifically mortality.
dailyEditorial.summary: This systematic review tackles the perennial question of whether implementing formal patient-to-nurse ratios in the emergency department translates to improved patient outcomes, specifically mortality. The authors synthesized literature from 2004 to 2025 across major databases to assess this association. While the general literature suggests that establishing nurse ratios benefits both patients and staff in various care settings, the applicability of these findings to the unique, high-acuity environment of the ED remains uncertain. The review's primary goal was to quantify the link between these ratios and ED mortality. However, the provided excerpt only details the methodology—the search strategy and the rigorous process of independent data extraction—without presenting the actual findings regarding mortality rates.

dailyEditorial.title: Whole Blood Not Better Than Components En Route to Hospital After Traumatic Injury
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When managing a trauma patient at high risk for hemorrhagic shock, you can consider whole, type O blood as an acceptable resuscitation fluid en route, as outcomes were comparable to component therapy per the TOWAR trial. However, remember that this suggests equivalence, not superiority, and the decision should remain guided by local protocols and institutional comfort level. Be mindful that this finding pertains specifically to the prehospital setting.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The TOWAR randomized trial provides data suggesting that for trauma patients anticipated to develop hemorrhagic shock, administering whole, type O blood en route to the hospital is comparably effective to administering…
dailyEditorial.summary: The TOWAR randomized trial provides data suggesting that for trauma patients anticipated to develop hemorrhagic shock, administering whole, type O blood en route to the hospital is comparably effective to administering specific blood components. The study's primary finding indicates that the clinical outcomes were similar between the two transfusion strategies. This challenges the long-standing preference for component therapy in the prehospital setting for major trauma. Essentially, the data supports the use of whole blood as a viable alternative to component-based resuscitation in this high-risk population.

dailyEditorial.title: HI-PEITHO Trial: Ultrasound-facilitated, Catheter Directed Fibrinolysis for PE
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For intermediate-risk PE, initial anticoagulation remains the cornerstone of management. Consider US-CDT plus anticoagulation if the patient is hemodynamically unstable or shows signs of impending decompensation, as this combination appears to mitigate acute hemodynamic decline. However, remember that this benefit is most pronounced in the sickest subset, and the decision to proceed requires careful risk stratification.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The HI-PEITHO trial was a randomized controlled trial designed to test whether adding ultrasound-facilitated, catheter-directed fibrinolysis (US-CDT) to standard anticoagulation improves outcomes for patients presenting…
dailyEditorial.summary: The HI-PEITHO trial was a randomized controlled trial designed to test whether adding ultrasound-facilitated, catheter-directed fibrinolysis (US-CDT) to standard anticoagulation improves outcomes for patients presenting with intermediate-risk pulmonary embolism. The primary question addressed was whether this combination therapy offers an advantage over anticoagulation alone in this specific patient cohort. The key finding highlighted is that the addition of US-CDT significantly reduces short-term hemodynamic decompensation, particularly in the most critically ill subset of intermediate-risk PE patients. While the trial suggests a benefit for this advanced intervention in select patients, the overall clinical management strategy remains anchored by initial anticoagulation. This suggests a nuanced role for thrombolysis adjuncts in the intermediate-risk setting.

dailyEditorial.title: Serratus Anterior Plane Block for Acute Rib Fractures in the Emergency Department: A Randomized Controlled Trial
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Given the significant improvement in PIC scores, consider adding SAPB to your standard analgesic regimen for acute rib fractures in the ED. It appears to be a safe and feasible adjunct that can improve immediate pain control. Remember that this is based on a single trial, so monitor for any procedural complications when implementing it.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This randomized controlled trial evaluated the utility of adding a Serratus Anterior Plane Block (SAPB) to standard analgesic management for acute rib fractures encountered in the emergency department.
dailyEditorial.summary: This randomized controlled trial evaluated the utility of adding a Serratus Anterior Plane Block (SAPB) to standard analgesic management for acute rib fractures encountered in the emergency department. The primary finding was that incorporating the SAPB significantly improved Pain Intensity Control (PIC) scores among this patient population. The authors emphasize that the procedure was performed safely by emergency physicians, suggesting it is a practical and scalable adjunct therapy. Overall, the data supports the integration of this regional anesthetic technique into the ED armamentarium for managing rib fracture pain.

dailyEditorial.title: 2025 guideline updates for community-acquired pneumonia diagnosis and management
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When managing stable CAP, remember the updated emphasis on potentially de-escalating antibiotics, especially if the patient is otherwise well and has a positive viral panel. You should also consider incorporating lung ultrasound if you have experienced personnel available. However, be mindful that these recommendations are guidelines, and the decision to initiate antibiotics should still be highly individualized.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The new 2025 American Thoracic Society guidelines offer updated recommendations for the diagnosis and management of community-acquired pneumonia (CAP), which is a frequent encounter in the ED.
dailyEditorial.summary: The new 2025 American Thoracic Society guidelines offer updated recommendations for the diagnosis and management of community-acquired pneumonia (CAP), which is a frequent encounter in the ED. These guidelines touch on imaging, antibiotic stewardship, and the role of corticosteroids. Notably, the document supports the integration of lung ultrasound by experienced clinicians into the diagnostic workup. Furthermore, it suggests a more nuanced approach to antibiotics, particularly for otherwise healthy patients who test positive for a virus. Clinically, the guidelines advocate for shortening antibiotic courses, recommending a minimum of three days for both stable outpatients and those admitted with nonsevere CAP.

dailyEditorial.title: Modified brain injury guidelines in a regional Australian setting indicate significant resource savings but also safety concerns
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When considering TBI management pathways, remember that while US-developed mBIG guidelines suggest resource savings without safety compromise, their local validation in an Australian setting is lacking. Therefore, while the concept of severity-based stratification is appealing for reducing unnecessary imaging or consultations, exercise caution and ensure local protocols account for regional differences before implementing significant guideline changes.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This paper addresses the significant resource utilization associated with traumatic brain injury (TBI) presentations in the emergency department, noting that while most cases are mild, current guidelines can lead to sub…
dailyEditorial.summary: This paper addresses the significant resource utilization associated with traumatic brain injury (TBI) presentations in the emergency department, noting that while most cases are mild, current guidelines can lead to substantial unnecessary neurosurgical consultations and imaging. The authors highlight the existence of modified brain injury guidelines (mBIG), which were developed in the US to safely stratify TBI severity and thereby reduce resource use without compromising patient outcomes. Crucially, the study points out that while these pathways have shown promise elsewhere, their applicability and safety profile within the specific context of regional Australia remain unvalidated. Given that TBI accounts for a notable portion of ED visits, understanding the local utility of such risk-stratification tools is important for optimizing care pathways.

dailyEditorial.title: Evaluating the TWIST score and point-of-care ultrasound for paediatric testicular torsion
dailyEditorial.takeaway: While the TWIST score and POCUS are highly accurate tools for risk stratification in the acute scrotum, do not rely on a negative score to rule out torsion. Given the persistent risk of false negatives, always maintain a high index of suspicion and consider proceeding with imaging or surgical consultation if the clinical picture remains concerning despite a low score.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This study assessed the diagnostic utility of the TWIST score combined with point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) for testicular torsion in children up to 15 years old, specifically when interpreted by emergency physicians o…
dailyEditorial.summary: This study assessed the diagnostic utility of the TWIST score combined with point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) for testicular torsion in children up to 15 years old, specifically when interpreted by emergency physicians or pediatricians. Overall, the findings suggest that both tools maintain high diagnostic accuracy in this setting, with the TWIST score showing a sensitivity of 91% and specificity of 95%. However, the authors caution that despite the high negative predictive value (99%), the non-negligible false-negative rate means the score should not be used as a definitive exclusionary tool. Furthermore, when defining positivity based on objective ultrasound findings like impaired blood flow or the whirlpool sign, the diagnostic metrics remain robust, though the positive predictive value is lower at 70%.

dailyEditorial.title: Journal update monthly top five
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When managing septic shock, remember that while mortality remains high, current guidelines lack a definitive, easily actionable risk stratification tool. This meta-analysis suggests focusing on the constellation of factors it reviews, as identifying high-risk patients early is crucial. However, treat this as a 'head turner' for now; it synthesizes existing data and may not yet mandate a change in immediate bedside practice.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This month's digest presents a curated look at five high-yield papers from outside of emergency medicine, synthesized by residents from South West England using a multimodal search approach.
dailyEditorial.summary: This month's digest presents a curated look at five high-yield papers from outside of emergency medicine, synthesized by residents from South West England using a multimodal search approach. A particularly noteworthy piece is a systematic review and meta-analysis focusing on prognostic factors for mortality in septic shock. The authors highlight that despite the persistent high mortality rate in this condition, there remains a significant gap in clear risk factor identification for predicting poor outcomes. This review synthesizes existing data to better delineate which clinical markers or physiological derangements predict the highest risk of death in septic patients. The overall utility of this synthesis is high, making it a key read for anyone managing critically ill septic patients.

dailyEditorial.title: Catheter-Directed Fibrinolysis for Pulmonary Embolism: Is It Old NEWS?
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For acute PE management, consider ultrasound-guided, catheter-directed fibrinolysis as a targeted alternative, especially if systemic thrombolysis is contraindicated or suboptimal. While the technique appears feasible, remember that this is a specialized intervention requiring advanced imaging and procedural expertise. Always weigh the benefits of localized lysis against the risks of incomplete clot burden assessment.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This article details the use of ultrasound-guided, catheter-directed fibrinolysis as a treatment strategy for acute pulmonary embolism.
dailyEditorial.summary: This article details the use of ultrasound-guided, catheter-directed fibrinolysis as a treatment strategy for acute pulmonary embolism. The authors present data on this minimally invasive approach, which aims to directly administer thrombolytic agents to the pulmonary embolus site. The technique leverages ultrasound guidance to improve visualization and targeted delivery, potentially offering an alternative to systemic thrombolysis or embolectomy. The discussion centers on the feasibility and safety profile of this localized fibrinolytic approach in the acute setting. It suggests a more controlled method for dissolving clot burden compared to systemic administration.

dailyEditorial.title: Management of patients with suspected but unidentified poisoning in the emergency department: a joint Royal College of Emergency Medicine and National Poisons Information Service best practice guideline
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When faced with an undifferentiated poisoned patient, adopt a systematic toxidromic approach by focusing on the expected clinical trajectory of potential toxins. Use this guideline to structure your initial workup and supportive measures, but always remember this is a general framework. Do not let it replace consulting the specific, detailed poison information available through TOXBASE or your local poisons centre.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This new joint guideline from the Royal College of Emergency Medicine and the National Poisons Information Service provides a comprehensive, generalized framework for managing patients in the ED where poisoning is suspe…
dailyEditorial.summary: This new joint guideline from the Royal College of Emergency Medicine and the National Poisons Information Service provides a comprehensive, generalized framework for managing patients in the ED where poisoning is suspected but not yet confirmed. The core of the approach advocated is a toxidromic strategy, meaning the management hinges on understanding the expected clinical course and toxicokinetics of potential agents rather than waiting for definitive identification. The guidance is designed to support the initial assessment process, helping clinicians systematically narrow down the differential diagnosis and guide immediate supportive care. It is crucial to recognize that this document is intended as a general clinical scaffold and explicitly states it does not supersede the detailed, poison-specific protocols found in TOXBASE or the NPIS. This holistic approach is valuable for maintaining systematic care when the toxicology picture is murky.

dailyEditorial.title: Point-of-care ultrasound by trained emergency physicians versus radiologists for deep vein thrombosis diagnosis: a prospective blinded study on diagnostic accuracy and time efficiency
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For suspected DVT, the high sensitivity and specificity achieved by trained ED physicians using POCUS suggest this is a reliable, rapid diagnostic tool. You can confidently utilize bedside ultrasound to triage and diagnose DVT, potentially avoiding the significant delay associated with formal radiology reads. Remember that while the accuracy is high, these findings suggest further research is needed to link this efficiency gain directly to improved patient flow or reduced length of stay.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This prospective, blinded study directly compared the diagnostic utility of compression ultrasonography performed by trained emergency physicians against the established standard of care, which was radiologist-performed…
dailyEditorial.summary: This prospective, blinded study directly compared the diagnostic utility of compression ultrasonography performed by trained emergency physicians against the established standard of care, which was radiologist-performed Doppler ultrasound, in patients with suspected deep vein thrombosis. The results were quite striking, showing that the emergency physicians achieved remarkably high diagnostic metrics, including a sensitivity of 97.0% and a specificity of 99.3%. Beyond accuracy, the time efficiency was a major finding; the median time-to-diagnosis was significantly shorter when performed at the bedside by the ED team compared to the median four hours required for the radiology department. Furthermore, the interobserver agreement across all evaluated venous segments was excellent, suggesting high concordance between the two groups. Overall, the data strongly support integrating bedside ultrasound into the diagnostic pathway for suspected DVT.

dailyEditorial.title: Presepsin for sepsis diagnosis in emergency departments: a multicentre study
dailyEditorial.takeaway: While presepsin showed a slight edge in diagnostic accuracy over PCT for sepsis and septic shock, remember that the overall predictive value remains modest. Do not let biomarker results dictate your management; they should only support your clinical suspicion when making critical decisions about resuscitation or antibiotics. Always integrate these results with the patient's full clinical picture.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This multicenter study evaluated the utility of presepsin and procalcitonin (PCT) for diagnosing sepsis and septic shock in the emergency department setting, using the established Sepsis-3 criteria.
dailyEditorial.summary: This multicenter study evaluated the utility of presepsin and procalcitonin (PCT) for diagnosing sepsis and septic shock in the emergency department setting, using the established Sepsis-3 criteria. The authors found that presepsin demonstrated slightly superior diagnostic accuracy compared to PCT for both sepsis and septic shock, as measured by AUROCs. Furthermore, elevated levels of both biomarkers were independently associated with increased 30-day mortality risk. While the biomarkers showed promise in differentiating septic patients, the overall diagnostic accuracy reported was modest. The key takeaway from the research is that these markers are best viewed as adjunct tools for early risk stratification rather than definitive diagnostic replacements for clinical acumen.

dailyEditorial.title: Should anticoagulants be initiated in patients with sepsis-induced new-onset atrial fibrillation? Best evidence topic report
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Do not initiate routine anticoagulation for AF in the setting of sepsis based on current evidence; the observational data is insufficient and somewhat contradictory regarding stroke benefit. If you must decide, weigh the low, unproven stroke benefit against the inherent bleeding risk, remembering that a definitive RCT is needed to guide practice. Proceed with caution and consider the underlying cause of the AF.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This topic report synthesizes the current evidence regarding the routine use of anticoagulation in patients who develop new-onset atrial fibrillation secondary to sepsis.
dailyEditorial.summary: This topic report synthesizes the current evidence regarding the routine use of anticoagulation in patients who develop new-onset atrial fibrillation secondary to sepsis. The review analyzed four observational studies, and the findings were not conclusive enough to recommend routine anticoagulation for stroke prevention in this specific group. Notably, none of the studies demonstrated a clear reduction in stroke risk associated with starting anticoagulation, and one large observational cohort even suggested a paradoxical increase in stroke incidence in those receiving AC. While bleeding risk wasn't consistently elevated across the literature, one study did report a potential benefit regarding reduced mortality with anticoagulation. Given these mixed and somewhat conflicting results, the authors emphasize that a definitive randomized controlled trial is necessary to clarify the true role of anticoagulation in this complex patient population.

dailyEditorial.title: Design Refinements for Sepsis Albumin Trials: Building on ICARUS-ED Experience
dailyEditorial.takeaway: The feasibility demonstrated in ICARUS-ED for enrolling sepsis patients in a real-time trial, including those from the ward, is a major operational win for future research. While the trial suggests albumin intervention is feasible in this setting, remember that the 95% infection confirmation rate is impressive but reflects the study's specific enrollment criteria. For now, this primarily informs trial design rather than immediate bedside protocol changes.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: Williams et al.
dailyEditorial.summary: Williams et al. present the results from the ICARUS-ED pilot randomized controlled trial, which explored the use of concentrated albumin for undifferentiated sepsis management directly within the emergency department setting. The authors highlight the significant operational achievement of the study: successfully conducting real-time sepsis trial enrollment with high protocol compliance, even incorporating ward-based patients alongside those destined for the ICU. Furthermore, the trial confirmed an infection diagnosis in 95% of the enrolled cohort, which is notable given the inherent diagnostic uncertainty encountered during the early stages of sepsis presentation. This work directly addresses the ongoing challenge of optimizing initial fluid resuscitation strategies in the critically ill patient presenting to the ED.

dailyEditorial.title: Validation of The Resuscitation Management (THERM) score in the emergency department
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When assessing critically ill patients in the ED, remember that the THERM score combines bicarbonate (max 22 mmol/L), GCS, and a blood pressure modifier to predict outcomes. While it showed better performance than NEWS in the original study, treat this as an adjunct tool; it should guide risk stratification but not replace clinical judgment, especially given the variability of its components.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This piece validates the Resuscitation Management (THERM) score, a specific prediction tool designed for critically ill patients encountered in the emergency department.
dailyEditorial.summary: This piece validates the Resuscitation Management (THERM) score, a specific prediction tool designed for critically ill patients encountered in the emergency department. The score is calculated using a combination of readily available parameters: the bicarbonate level (capped at 22 mmol/L), the total Glasgow Coma Scale, and a deduction of 4 if the systolic blood pressure is low. The authors report that in their initial validation, THERM demonstrated significantly superior performance compared to established tools like the National Early Warning Score (NEWS), even considering its updated iterations. This suggests that incorporating these specific physiological markers into a composite score may offer enhanced prognostic capability for resource allocation in the resuscitation bay.

dailyEditorial.title: Systematic Review of Pediatric Ketamine in Emergency Department Procedural Sedation: Frequency and Predictors of Adverse Events
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When considering ketamine for pediatric procedural sedation, remember that pooled data from large cohorts (≥250 subjects) are needed to accurately gauge the true frequency of adverse events. While the review inventories predictors, the clinical utility of these predictors needs careful interpretation, as the evidence base for their individual importance might be variable. Proceed with standard monitoring protocols, but remain mindful that the literature synthesis itself highlights the need for standardized reporting across studies.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This systematic review synthesizes existing literature to provide a comprehensive overview of adverse events associated with using intravenous ketamine for procedural sedation in the pediatric emergency department.
dailyEditorial.summary: This systematic review synthesizes existing literature to provide a comprehensive overview of adverse events associated with using intravenous ketamine for procedural sedation in the pediatric emergency department. The authors specifically focused on pooling data from studies involving a substantial number of pediatric subjects, ideally those with at least 250 participants, to establish more robust frequency estimates for these complications. Beyond just listing adverse events, the review also takes time to catalog and assess the clinical significance of any predictor variables that other studies have identified in this setting. This synthesis is valuable because it attempts to move beyond anecdotal reports to give a more quantitative picture of ketamine safety in this common ED procedure.

dailyEditorial.title: Predictive criteria for oesophageal perforation in patients with pneumomediastinum: can invasive diagnostic modalities be avoided?
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When you see PNM on CT, don't automatically jump to endoscopy. Focus on risk stratification: if the patient is asymptomatic and the CT lacks signs like mediastinal fluid or esophageal wall disruption, conservative management is likely safe. Remember that dysphagia and vomiting, coupled with those specific CT findings, are the key indicators warranting further invasive workup.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This retrospective analysis tackles the common diagnostic dilemma arising from the increased incidental detection of pneumomediastinum (PNM) on CT scans, specifically concerning the need for invasive evaluation for esop…
dailyEditorial.summary: This retrospective analysis tackles the common diagnostic dilemma arising from the increased incidental detection of pneumomediastinum (PNM) on CT scans, specifically concerning the need for invasive evaluation for esophageal perforation (OP). The authors built a predictive model using clinical, biochemical, and radiological data from patients with confirmed PNM. They found that the presence of dysphagia and vomiting, when combined with specific CT findings like mediastinal fluid, pleural effusion, and visible esophageal wall disruption, are strong predictors of underlying OP. Crucially, the study suggests that patients presenting with PNM who are otherwise asymptomatic and lack these high-risk radiological or clinical signs can likely be managed conservatively, potentially avoiding unnecessary endoscopy.

dailyEditorial.title: Percutaneous sigmoid fixation for management of recurrent sigmoid volvulus: a randomized controlled clinical trial
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For patients presenting with recurrent sigmoid volvulus, percutaneous endoscopic fixation appears to be a compelling, lower-morbidity option that avoids general anesthesia. Consider this approach when the patient is deemed stable enough for endoscopy, as it may reduce operative risks compared to traditional surgical interventions. However, always weigh the benefits against the potential for failed fixation or complications related to the underlying bowel pathology.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This randomized controlled trial directly compares percutaneous endoscopic fixation against other management strategies for recurrent sigmoid volvulus.
dailyEditorial.summary: This randomized controlled trial directly compares percutaneous endoscopic fixation against other management strategies for recurrent sigmoid volvulus. The authors conclude that this minimally invasive technique represents a viable alternative that is associated with both lower morbidity and lower mortality rates. A key advantage highlighted is that this approach can be performed without the need for general anesthesia, which is a significant consideration in the acute setting. While the study provides supportive data, it is important to remember that the evidence base for this specific intervention needs to be weighed against the risks associated with the underlying condition and the patient's overall clinical status. Overall, the findings suggest a shift toward less invasive management options for this recurrent issue.

dailyEditorial.title: A Woman With Rapidly Progressive Weakness
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When encountering acute, progressive, flaccid paralysis with a distinct sensory level in a patient with CKD, immediately consider the differential diagnosis beyond simple Guillain-Barré syndrome. The combination of profound weakness, areflexia, and specific sensory deficits mandates ruling out metabolic derangements or compressive neuropathies. Always ensure a comprehensive workup including electrolytes, renal function, and potentially nerve conduction studies.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This case report details a 57-year-old woman with known chronic kidney disease who presented with acute, rapidly progressive, and nontraumatic lower extremity weakness and numbness.
dailyEditorial.summary: This case report details a 57-year-old woman with known chronic kidney disease who presented with acute, rapidly progressive, and nontraumatic lower extremity weakness and numbness. On examination, she demonstrated profound flaccid paralysis in the legs, absent reflexes, and a distinct pattern of sensory loss affecting pain and temperature below the nipple-to-xiphoid level, alongside bowel and bladder incontinence. The presentation is highly suggestive of an acute neuromuscular process, and the authors highlight the importance of considering underlying systemic or metabolic causes in the setting of rapidly evolving weakness, especially in a patient with CKD. The clinical picture warrants a thorough workup to differentiate between peripheral nerve, root, or neuromuscular junction pathology.

dailyEditorial.title: The infusion after the bolus: a quality improvement programme to support emergency department airway governance in Ireland
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Implementing a formal, multi-component airway governance program—including designated leadership, standardized checklists, and mandatory daily simulation—appears effective for maintaining high first-pass success rates. While the reported metrics are encouraging, remember that this was a structured QI effort; the key takeaway is the *system* supporting the procedure, not just the individual skill. Consider integrating similar structured drills and leadership roles into your local protocol to sustain performance.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This quality improvement project details the implementation of a structured airway governance framework within a large Irish Emergency Department to standardize and improve intubation practices.
dailyEditorial.summary: This quality improvement project details the implementation of a structured airway governance framework within a large Irish Emergency Department to standardize and improve intubation practices. The intervention was multi-faceted, incorporating the designation of an airway lead, mandatory use of standardized checklists, and routine daily intubation drills, alongside data capture via a registry. The authors reported that across 156 intubations, the program successfully maintained a first-pass success rate of 91.7% and a complication rate of 12.3%. The core finding suggests that a combination of leadership, checklist adherence, simulation, and continuous feedback is effective for sustaining high procedural competency. This framework is presented as a pragmatic and replicable model for national airway governance.
